title: The Clock That Runs Five Minutes Slow
text_zh: 我家客厅墙上挂着一个老式挂钟，走了十几年，慢了五分钟，再也调不回来了。修表的师傅说零件老化了，要修不如换一个新的。但我一直没换。原因很简单：我已经习惯了那五分钟的偏差。每天早上看一眼钟，心里自动加五分钟，反而比直接看准确时间多了一个缓冲。出门前觉得还有五分钟，不那么着急。吃饭的时候看一眼，觉得时间还早，不用狼吞虎咽。有一次妻子忍不住买了一个新钟挂在餐厅，电波对时的，精确到秒。奇怪的是，自从有了那个精确的钟，我反而觉得时间过得更快了。每次一看，已经比我以为的晚了五分钟，于是总是在赶。后来我跟妻子商量，把新钟挂到了书房，客厅还是留那个慢五分钟的。她笑我说：「你不是在看时间，你是在看一个让你舒服的数字。」我想了想说：「也许吧。但人活着，不就是在找一个让自己舒服的节奏吗？」
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā kètīng qiáng shàng guà zhe yī gè lǎoshì guàzhōng, zǒu le shí jǐ nián, màn le wǔ fēnzhōng, zài yě tiáo bù huílái le. Xiūbiǎo de shīfu shuō língjiàn lǎohuà le, yào xiū bùrú huàn yī gè xīn de. Dàn wǒ yīzhí méi huàn. Yuányīn hěn jiǎndān: wǒ yǐjīng xíguàn le nà wǔ fēnzhōng de piānchā. Měi tiān zǎoshang kàn yī yǎn zhōng, xīnlǐ zìdòng jiā wǔ fēnzhōng, fǎn'ér bǐ zhíjiē kàn zhǔnquè shíjiān duō le yī gè huǎnchōng. Chūmén qián juéde hái yǒu wǔ fēnzhōng, bú nàme zhāojí. Chīfàn de shíhou kàn yī yǎn, juéde shíjiān hái zǎo, búyòng lángtūnhǔyàn. Yǒu yī cì qīzi rěn bú zhù mǎi le yī gè xīn zhōng guà zài cāntīng, diànbō duìshí de, jīngquè dào miǎo. Qíguài de shì, zìcóng yǒu le nàge jīngquè de zhōng, wǒ fǎn'ér juéde shíjiān guò de gèng kuài le. Měi cì yī kàn, yǐjīng bǐ wǒ yǐwéi de wǎn le wǔ fēnzhōng, yúshì zǒng shì zài gǎn. Hòulái wǒ gēn qīzi shāngliang, bǎ xīn zhōng guà dào le shūfáng, kètīng háishi liú nàge màn wǔ fēnzhōng de. Tā xiào wǒ shuō: 「Nǐ bú shì zài kàn shíjiān, nǐ shì zài kàn yī gè ràng nǐ shūfu de shùzì.」Wǒ xiǎng le xiǎng shuō: 「Yěxǔ ba. Dàn rén huó zhe, bú jiùshì zài zhǎo yī gè ràng zìjǐ shūfu de jiézòu ma?」
text_en: On the living room wall hangs an old clock that has been running for over a decade. It's five minutes slow now, and can't be adjusted back. The watch repairman said the parts have aged—better to buy a new one than fix it. But I never replaced it. The reason is simple: I've grown accustomed to that five-minute offset. Each morning I glance at the clock, mentally add five minutes, and it actually gives me more of a buffer than seeing the exact time. Before heading out, I feel I still have five minutes, so I'm not as rushed. At mealtimes, a glance tells me it's still early, so I don't have to wolf down my food. Once my wife couldn't help buying a new clock for the dining room—radio-synchronized, accurate to the second. Strangely, ever since we got that precise clock, time seemed to pass faster. Every time I looked, it was already five minutes later than I'd thought, so I was always rushing. Later I discussed it with my wife and moved the new clock to the study, keeping the five-minutes-slow one in the living room. She laughed and said: 'You're not checking the time—you're looking at a number that makes you comfortable.' I thought about it and said: 'Maybe. But isn't living just about finding a rhythm that feels right?'

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么不换掉那个慢五分钟的钟？
questions.q_en: Why doesn't the author replace the clock that runs five minutes slow?

options.pinyin: tā mǎi bù qǐ xīn zhōng

options.text_en: He's grown accustomed to the offset—it makes his pace of life more comfortable

options.text: 那是一个有纪念意义的礼物
options.pinyin: nà shì yī gè yǒu jìniàn yìyì de lǐwù
options.text_en: It's a meaningful gift

options.pinyin: tā juéde lǎo zhōng gèng hǎokàn
options.text_en: He thinks the old clock looks better

questions.q_en: How did the author feel after getting the precise new clock?

options.pinyin: gèng fàngsōng le
options.text_en: More relaxed

options.text: 时间过得更快，总是在赶
options.pinyin: shíjiān guò de gèng kuài, zǒng shì zài gǎn
options.text_en: Time felt faster, always rushing

options.pinyin: gèng zhǔnshí le
options.text_en: More punctual

options.pinyin: méiyǒu shénme biànhuà
options.text_en: No change

questions.q_zh: 妻子说「你不是在看时间，你是在看一个让你舒服的数字」，这说明了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the wife's remark reveal?

options.pinyin: zuòzhě shùxué bù hǎo
options.text_en: The author is bad at math

options.text: 人们有时候选择「不那么精确」是为了心理上的舒适
options.pinyin: rénmen yǒu shíhou xuǎnzé 「bú nàme jīngquè」shì wèile xīnlǐ shàng de shūshì
options.text_en: People sometimes choose 'less accuracy' for psychological comfort

options.pinyin: qīzi juéde zuòzhě tài lǎn le
options.text_en: The wife thinks the author is too lazy

options.pinyin: shíjiān shì xiāngduì de
options.text_en: Time is relative

title: The Convenience Store at Three AM
text_zh: 我住的地方附近有一家二十四小时便利店。白天它和别的便利店没什么区别，但凌晨三点的它，有一种完全不同的气质。灯光是一样的白，货架是一样的满，但整个店里只有收银台后面的店员和偶尔进来的一两个顾客。那个时间段来的人都有自己的故事：加班到深夜的程序员，买一盒泡面和一罐啤酒；出租车司机进来买一包烟，站在门口抽完再走；偶尔有喝醉的年轻人进来买水，靠着冰柜坐一会儿清醒一下。我有一段时间失眠很严重，经常凌晨两三点出门散步，最后总会走到这家便利店。进去买一瓶水，不急着走，就站在杂志架前面翻一翻。店员是个二十出头的女孩，从来不催我，也不多说什么，偶尔会说一句：「今天外面冷不冷？」就这一句话，在凌晨三点听起来特别暖。后来失眠好了，我就再也没在那个时间去过。但我一直记得那种感觉：一座巨大的城市在睡觉，只有一盏灯还醒着，等着那些无处可去的人。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhù de dìfang fùjìn yǒu yī jiā èrshísì xiǎoshí biànlìdiàn. Báitiān tā hé bié de biànlìdiàn méi shénme qūbié, dàn língchén sān diǎn de tā, yǒu yī zhǒng wánquán bùtóng de qìzhì. Dēngguāng shì yīyàng de bái, huòjià shì yīyàng de mǎn, dàn zhěnggè diàn lǐ zhǐ yǒu shōuyíntái hòumiàn de diànyuán hé ǒu'ěr jìnlái de yī liǎng gè gùkè. Nàge shíjiānduàn lái de rén dōu yǒu zìjǐ de gùshi: jiābān dào shēnyè de chéngxùyuán, mǎi yī hé pàomiàn hé yī guàn píjiǔ; chūzūchē sījī jìnlái mǎi yī bāo yān, zhàn zài ménkǒu chōu wán zài zǒu; ǒu'ěr yǒu hē zuì de niánqīngrén jìnlái mǎi shuǐ, kào zhe bīngguì zuò yīhuìr qīngxǐng yīxià. Wǒ yǒu yī duàn shíjiān shīmián hěn yánzhòng, jīngcháng língchén liǎng sān diǎn chūmén sànbù, zuìhòu zǒng huì zǒu dào zhè jiā biànlìdiàn. Jìnqù mǎi yī píng shuǐ, bù jí zhe zǒu, jiù zhàn zài zázhì jià qiánmiàn fān yī fān. Diànyuán shì gè èrshí chūtóu de nǚhái, cónglái bù cuī wǒ, yě bù duō shuō shénme, ǒu'ěr huì shuō yī jù: 「Jīntiān wàimiàn lěng bù lěng?」Jiù zhè yī jù huà, zài língchén sān diǎn tīng qǐlái tèbié nuǎn. Hòulái shīmián hǎo le, wǒ jiù zài yě méi zài nàge shíjiān qù guò. Dàn wǒ yīzhí jìde nà zhǒng gǎnjué: yī zuò jùdà de chéngshì zài shuìjiào, zhǐ yǒu yī zhǎn dēng hái xǐng zhe, děng zhe nàxiē wú chù kě qù de rén.
text_en: Near where I live there's a 24-hour convenience store. During the day it's no different from any other, but at 3 AM it has an entirely different quality. The lights are the same white, the shelves the same full, but the entire store holds only the clerk behind the register and the occasional customer or two. Everyone who comes at that hour has their own story: the programmer working overtime until deep night, buying a cup of instant noodles and a can of beer; the taxi driver popping in for a pack of cigarettes, smoking one by the door before leaving; occasionally a drunk young person coming in for water, leaning against the freezer for a moment to sober up. For a while my insomnia was severe, and I often went out walking at 2 or 3 AM, always ending up at this convenience store. I'd go in, buy a bottle of water, not rush to leave, just stand by the magazine rack and browse. The clerk was a girl barely past twenty. She never hurried me, never said much—just occasionally: 'Is it cold outside today?' That single sentence, heard at 3 AM, felt extraordinarily warm. Later my insomnia got better, and I never went at that hour again. But I've always remembered that feeling: an enormous city sleeping, with just one light still awake, waiting for those who have nowhere else to go.

questions.q_zh: 凌晨三点的便利店和白天有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How is the convenience store at 3 AM different from during the day?

options.pinyin: dēngguāng gèng àn, shāngpǐn gèng shǎo

options.text: 外表一样，但气氛完全不同，顾客都有各自的深夜故事
options.pinyin: wàibiǎo yīyàng, dàn qìfēn wánquán bùtóng, gùkè dōu yǒu gèzì de shēnyè gùshi

options.pinyin: yīnwèi wàimiàn quèshí hěn lěng

options.text: 在深夜无人的时刻，一句简单的关心就足以让人感到被在意
options.pinyin: zài shēnyè wú rén de shíkè, yī jù jiǎndān de guānxīn jiù zúyǐ ràng rén gǎndào bèi zàiyì
options.text_en: In the quiet emptiness of deep night, a simple expression of care is enough to make someone feel noticed

options.pinyin: diànyuán shì tā de péngyou
options.text_en: The clerk is his friend

options.pinyin: biànlìdiàn lǐ yǒu nuǎnqì
options.text_en: The convenience store has heating

questions.q_zh: 作者说「一盏灯还醒着，等着那些无处可去的人」，表达了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author express with 'one light still awake, waiting for those who have nowhere else to go'?

options.text: 便利店应该延长营业时间
options.pinyin: biànlìdiàn yīnggāi yáncháng yíngyè shíjiān
options.text_en: Convenience stores should extend their hours

options.pinyin: chéngshì xūyào gèng duō de lùdēng

options.text: 深夜便利店是城市里孤独的人最后的庇护所
options.pinyin: shēnyè biànlìdiàn shì chéngshì lǐ gūdú de rén zuìhòu de bìhùsuǒ
options.text_en: The late-night convenience store is a last refuge for lonely people in the city

options.text: 失眠的人应该多出门走走
options.pinyin: shīmián de rén yīnggāi duō chūmén zǒu zou
options.text_en: Insomniacs should go out and walk more

title: The Cafeteria Auntie's Memory
text_zh: 大学食堂二楼打菜窗口的刘阿姨，有一个让所有学生都觉得不可思议的能力：她能记住常来的学生喜欢吃什么。你排队到她面前，还没开口，她就已经在给你打饭了：「老样子，一荤一素加个蛋，对吧？」刚开始大家觉得惊讶，后来就习惯了，有人甚至故意不开口，看她猜得准不准。她说她每天要服务几百个学生，但真正「记住」的只有那些连续来吃了一个学期以上的人。「脸我不一定记得清，但手我记得，」她说，「每个人拿餐盘的姿势不一样，有人用左手，有人把筷子架在盘子上。」每年六月毕业季的时候，刘阿姨会特别安静。她说那几天来吃饭的人突然少了一大半，但她的手还是会不自觉地按照消失了的那些人的口味去打菜。「习惯这个东西很奇怪，人走了，但动作还在，」她说这话的时候笑着，但眼睛里有一点湿。我想，这大概就是一种属于食堂阿姨的告别方式——不是说再见，而是在无人的窗口前，继续为已经离开的人打一份他们爱吃的菜。
text_pinyin: Dàxué shítáng èr lóu dǎ cài chuāngkǒu de Liú āyí, yǒu yī gè ràng suǒyǒu xuéshēng dōu juéde bùkěsīyì de nénglì: tā néng jì zhù cháng lái de xuéshēng xǐhuan chī shénme. Nǐ páiduì dào tā miànqián, hái méi kāikǒu, tā jiù yǐjīng zài gěi nǐ dǎ fàn le: 「Lǎo yàngzi, yī hūn yī sù jiā gè dàn, duì ba?」Gāng kāishǐ dàjiā juéde jīngyà, hòulái jiù xíguàn le, yǒu rén shènzhì gùyì bù kāikǒu, kàn tā cāi de zhǔn bù zhǔn. Tā shuō tā měi tiān yào fúwù jǐ bǎi gè xuéshēng, dàn zhēnzhèng 「jì zhù」de zhǐ yǒu nàxiē liánxù lái chī le yī gè xuéqī yǐshàng de rén. 「Liǎn wǒ bù yīdìng jì de qīng, dàn shǒu wǒ jìde,」tā shuō, 「měi gè rén ná cānpán de zīshì bù yīyàng, yǒu rén yòng zuǒshǒu, yǒu rén bǎ kuàizi jià zài pánzi shàng.」Měi nián liùyuè bìyè jì de shíhou, Liú āyí huì tèbié ānjìng. Tā shuō nà jǐ tiān lái chīfàn de rén tūrán shǎo le yī dà bàn, dàn tā de shǒu háishi huì bù zìjué de ànzhào xiāoshī le de nàxiē rén de kǒuwèi qù dǎ cài. 「Xíguàn zhège dōngxi hěn qíguài, rén zǒu le, dàn dòngzuò hái zài,」tā shuō zhè huà de shíhou xiào zhe, dàn yǎnjīng lǐ yǒu yīdiǎn shī. Wǒ xiǎng, zhè dàgài jiùshì yī zhǒng shǔyú shítáng āyí de gàobié fāngshì——bú shì shuō zàijiàn, ér shì zài wú rén de chuāngkǒu qián, jìxù wèi yǐjīng líkāi de rén dǎ yī fèn tāmen ài chī de cài.
text_en: Auntie Liu at the second-floor serving window of the university cafeteria has an ability that all the students find incredible: she remembers what the regulars like to eat. You queue up to her window and before you've opened your mouth, she's already serving you: 'The usual—one meat, one vegetable, plus an egg, right?' At first everyone was amazed; later they got used to it. Some even deliberately stayed silent to see if she'd guess correctly. She said she serves hundreds of students daily, but truly 'remembers' only those who've been coming continuously for at least a semester. 'I don't necessarily remember faces clearly, but I remember hands,' she said. 'Everyone holds their tray differently—some use the left hand, some rest chopsticks across the plate.' Every June during graduation season, Auntie Liu becomes particularly quiet. She says the number of diners suddenly drops by half, but her hands still unconsciously serve portions matching the tastes of those who've vanished. 'Habit is a strange thing—the person is gone, but the motions remain,' she said this smiling, but with a hint of moisture in her eyes. I think this is perhaps a cafeteria auntie's way of saying goodbye—not with words, but by continuing to serve a favorite dish for someone who has already left, at an empty window.

questions.q_zh: 刘阿姨记住学生的方式和普通人有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How does Auntie Liu's way of remembering students differ from most people's?

options.pinyin: tā jì zhù měi gè rén de míngzì

options.pinyin: tā zhǔyào tōngguò shǒu hé ná cānpán de zīshì lái shíbié rén

options.text: 她给每个学生编了号码

options.pinyin: tā pāi le zhàopiàn

questions.q_zh: 毕业季刘阿姨为什么特别安静？

questions.q_zh: 「人走了，但动作还在」这句话揭示了什么？
questions.q_en: What does 'the person is gone, but the motions remain' reveal?

options.text: 刘阿姨的工作太机械了
options.pinyin: Liú āyí de gōngzuò tài jīxiè le
options.text_en: Auntie Liu's work is too mechanical

options.text: 身体的习惯比意识的记忆更持久，是一种无声的情感表达
options.pinyin: shēntǐ de xíguàn bǐ yìshi de jìyì gèng chíjiǔ, shì yī zhǒng wúshēng de qínggǎn biǎodá
options.text_en: Bodily habit is more enduring than conscious memory, and is a silent expression of emotion

options.text: 食堂应该用电子点餐系统
options.pinyin: shítáng yīnggāi yòng diànzǐ diǎncān xìtǒng

options.text: 学生不应该每天吃一样的菜
options.pinyin: xuéshēng bù yīnggāi měi tiān chī yīyàng de cài
options.text_en: Students shouldn't eat the same dishes every day

title: The Flowers That Appear Every Monday
text_zh: 我们办公楼一楼的大厅里有一个花瓶，每周一早上都会出现一束新鲜的花。没有人知道是谁放的。前台的小陈说她每周一早上七点到岗的时候，花已经在那里了。一开始大家以为是物业统一采购的，但物业说不是。有人猜是某个浪漫的男同事在追求前台的小陈，但小陈说花瓶在她来这里工作之前就有了，这个习惯已经持续了至少三年。有好奇的同事试过提前来看，六点半到的时候花已经放好了。有人甚至想过装摄像头，但被大家否决了——理由是：「万一知道了是谁，反而没意思了。」三年里，花的种类一直在变：春天多是百合和康乃馨，夏天是向日葵和雏菊，秋天是菊花和桔梗，冬天则是腊梅和山茶花。无论什么季节都是应时的花。这说明放花的人不是随便买的，而是每一周都认真选过。我后来不再追究这件事了。有些谜不需要答案。那束花每周一安静地出现在大厅里，像一个不需要回应的问候——它只是想让你知道，有人在乎这个空间是不是好看的。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen bàngōnglóu yī lóu de dàtīng lǐ yǒu yī gè huāpíng, měi zhōuyī zǎoshang dōu huì chūxiàn yī shù xīnxiān de huā. Méiyǒu rén zhīdào shì shuí fàng de. Qiántái de xiǎo Chén shuō tā měi zhōuyī zǎoshang qī diǎn dào gǎng de shíhou, huā yǐjīng zài nàlǐ le. Yī kāishǐ dàjiā yǐwéi shì wùyè tǒngyī cǎigòu de, dàn wùyè shuō bú shì. Yǒu rén cāi shì mǒu gè làngmàn de nán tóngshì zài zhuīqiú qiántái de xiǎo Chén, dàn xiǎo Chén shuō huāpíng zài tā lái zhèlǐ gōngzuò zhīqián jiù yǒu le, zhège xíguàn yǐjīng chíxù le zhìshǎo sān nián. Yǒu hàoqí de tóngshì shì guò tíqián lái kàn, liù diǎn bàn dào de shíhou huā yǐjīng fàng hǎo le. Yǒu rén shènzhì xiǎng guò zhuāng shèxiàngtóu, dàn bèi dàjiā fǒujué le——lǐyóu shì: 「Wànyī zhīdào le shì shuí, fǎn'ér méi yìsi le.」Sān nián lǐ, huā de zhǒnglèi yīzhí zài biàn: chūntiān duō shì bǎihé hé kāngnǎixīn, xiàtiān shì xiàngrìkuí hé chújú, qiūtiān shì júhuā hé jiégěng, dōngtiān zé shì làméi hé shānchá huā. Wúlùn shénme jìjié dōu shì yìngshí de huā. Zhè shuōmíng fàng huā de rén bú shì suíbiàn mǎi de, ér shì měi yī zhōu dōu rènzhēn xuǎn guò. Wǒ hòulái bú zài zhuījiù zhè jiàn shì le. Yǒuxiē mí bù xūyào dá'àn. Nà shù huā měi zhōuyī ānjìng de chūxiàn zài dàtīng lǐ, xiàng yī gè bù xūyào huíyìng de wènhòu——tā zhǐshì xiǎng ràng nǐ zhīdào, yǒu rén zàihū zhège kōngjiān shì bú shì hǎokàn de.
text_en: In the ground-floor lobby of our office building there's a vase, and every Monday morning a fresh bouquet appears in it. Nobody knows who puts it there. Xiao Chen at reception says when she arrives at seven on Mondays, the flowers are already there. At first everyone assumed it was a bulk purchase by property management, but management said no. Someone guessed a romantic male colleague was courting Xiao Chen, but she said the vase predated her—the habit had been going on for at least three years. A curious colleague tried coming early; at 6:30 the flowers were already placed. Someone even suggested installing a camera, but everyone vetoed it—the reasoning being: 'If we find out who it is, it won't be interesting anymore.' Over three years, the flower varieties kept changing: lilies and carnations in spring, sunflowers and daisies in summer, chrysanthemums and bellflowers in autumn, wintersweet and camellias in winter. Always seasonal. This showed the person wasn't buying randomly but carefully choosing each week. Eventually I stopped investigating. Some mysteries don't need answers. Those flowers appearing quietly in the lobby every Monday are like a greeting that expects no reply—just wanting you to know that someone cares whether this space is beautiful.

questions.q_zh: 为什么同事们否决了装摄像头的提议？
questions.q_en: Why did the colleagues veto the proposal to install a camera?

options.pinyin: zhuāng shèxiàngtóu tài guì le
options.text_en: Installing cameras is too expensive

options.text: 他们觉得知道答案反而会失去趣味
options.pinyin: tāmen juéde zhīdào dá'àn fǎn'ér huì shīqù qùwèi
options.text_en: They felt knowing the answer would actually take away the charm

options.pinyin: wùyè bù yǔnxǔ
options.text_en: Property management wouldn't allow it

options.pinyin: tāmen yǐjīng zhīdào shì shuí le
options.text_en: They already knew who it was

questions.q_zh: 花的种类随季节变化说明了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the changing variety of flowers by season indicate?

options.pinyin: huādiàn zhǐ mài dāngjì de huā
options.text_en: Flower shops only sell seasonal flowers

options.text: 放花的人每周都用心挑选，不是随意行为
options.pinyin: fàng huā de rén měi zhōu dōu yòngxīn tiāoxuǎn, bú shì suíyì xíngwéi
options.text_en: The person carefully selects each week—it's not a casual act

options.pinyin: bùtóng de rén zài fàng huā
options.text_en: Different people are placing the flowers

options.pinyin: fàng huā de rén shì huādiàn lǎobǎn
options.text_en: The person placing flowers is a flower shop owner

questions.q_zh: 作者最后为什么不再追究这件事？
questions.q_en: Why does the author ultimately stop investigating?

options.pinyin: tā duì huā bù gǎn xìngqù le
options.text_en: He lost interest in the flowers

options.text: 他认为有些美好的事物不需要解释，保持神秘反而更好
options.pinyin: tā rènwéi yǒuxiē měihǎo de shìwù bù xūyào jiěshì, bǎochí shénmì fǎn'ér gèng hǎo
options.text_en: He believes some beautiful things don't need explanations—the mystery is better preserved

options.text: 他找到了答案但不想告诉别人
options.pinyin: tā zhǎo dào le dá'àn dàn bù xiǎng gàosu biéren
options.text_en: He found the answer but doesn't want to tell others

options.text: 前台的小陈让他别管了
options.pinyin: qiántái de xiǎo Chén ràng tā bié guǎn le
options.text_en: Xiao Chen at reception told him to drop it

title: The Old Man Who Feeds Stray Cats
text_zh: 每天傍晚六点半，小区花园的角落会出现一位穿灰色夹克的老人，手里提着一个塑料袋，里面装着猫粮。他把猫粮倒在花坛边的几个纸碗里，然后坐在旁边的石头上等。不一会儿，三四只流浪猫就会从各个方向慢慢走过来。它们不急着吃，先远远地看一看，确认是那个老人以后才靠近。老人从来不伸手摸它们，也不叫它们。他就那样安静地坐着，看它们吃完，然后收拾好纸碗，起身离开。我观察了他大半年，几乎每天都是这样。有一次下大雨，我以为他不会来了，结果他打着伞还是来了。那天只来了一只猫，淋得湿透的。老人把伞倾斜过去遮住猫，自己的半边身子露在雨里。后来我终于忍不住跟他搭话。他说他老伴两年前走了，儿女都在外地。「猫不会问你为什么难过，」他说，「它们只是来吃东西。但它们每天都来，这就够了。」我忽然意识到，也许不是他在喂猫，而是猫在陪他——每天傍晚六点半，准时赴一个无声的约定。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān bàngwǎn liù diǎn bàn, xiǎoqū huāyuán de jiǎoluò huì chūxiàn yī wèi chuān huīsè jiákè de lǎorén, shǒu lǐ tí zhe yī gè sùliào dài, lǐmiàn zhuāng zhe māoliáng. Tā bǎ māoliáng dào zài huātán biān de jǐ gè zhǐwǎn lǐ, ránhòu zuò zài pángbiān de shítou shàng děng. Bù yīhuìr, sān sì zhī liúlàng māo jiù huì cóng gè gè fāngxiàng mànmàn zǒu guòlái. Tāmen bù jí zhe chī, xiān yuǎnyuǎn de kàn yī kàn, quèrèn shì nàge lǎorén yǐhòu cái kàojìn. Lǎorén cónglái bù shēnshǒu mō tāmen, yě bú jiào tāmen. Tā jiù nàyàng ānjìng de zuò zhe, kàn tāmen chī wán, ránhòu shōushi hǎo zhǐwǎn, qǐshēn líkāi. Wǒ guānchá le tā dà bàn nián, jīhū měi tiān dōu shì zhèyàng. Yǒu yī cì xià dà yǔ, wǒ yǐwéi tā bú huì lái le, jiéguǒ tā dǎ zhe sǎn háishi lái le. Nà tiān zhǐ lái le yī zhī māo, lín de shī tòu de. Lǎorén bǎ sǎn qīngxié guòqù zhē zhù māo, zìjǐ de bàn biān shēnzi lù zài yǔ lǐ. Hòulái wǒ zhōngyú rěn bú zhù gēn tā dā huà. Tā shuō tā lǎobàn liǎng nián qián zǒu le, érnǚ dōu zài wàidì. 「Māo bú huì wèn nǐ wèishéme nánguò,」tā shuō, 「tāmen zhǐshì lái chī dōngxi. Dàn tāmen měi tiān dōu lái, zhè jiù gòu le.」Wǒ hūrán yìshi dào, yěxǔ bú shì tā zài wèi māo, ér shì māo zài péi tā——měi tiān bàngwǎn liù diǎn bàn, zhǔnshí fù yī gè wúshēng de yuēdìng.
text_en: Every evening at 6:30, an old man in a grey jacket appears in the corner of the compound's garden, carrying a plastic bag of cat food. He pours the food into a few paper bowls by the flower bed, then sits on a nearby rock and waits. Before long, three or four stray cats approach slowly from different directions. They're in no rush to eat—first they look from afar, and only after confirming it's that old man do they draw close. The old man never reaches out to pet them, never calls them. He just sits there quietly, watches them finish, then tidies up the bowls and leaves. I observed him for over half a year; it was like this almost every day. Once during a heavy downpour, I assumed he wouldn't come, but he showed up with an umbrella. That day only one cat came, soaked through. The old man tilted the umbrella over the cat, leaving half his own body exposed to the rain. Eventually I couldn't resist striking up a conversation. He said his wife had passed two years ago; his children were all in other cities. 'Cats won't ask you why you're sad,' he said. 'They just come to eat. But they come every day, and that's enough.' I suddenly realized that maybe he wasn't feeding the cats—the cats were keeping him company. Every evening at 6:30, faithfully keeping a silent appointment.

questions.q_zh: 流浪猫靠近老人之前会先做什么？
questions.q_en: What do the stray cats do before approaching the old man?

options.pinyin: lìkè pǎo guòlái chī dōngxi
options.text_en: Run over immediately to eat

options.text: 先远远观察，确认是那个老人后才靠近
options.pinyin: xiān yuǎnyuǎn guānchá, quèrèn shì nàge lǎorén hòu cái kàojìn
options.text_en: First observe from afar, and approach only after confirming it's that old man

options.pinyin: jiào jǐ shēng biǎoshì gǎnxiè
options.text_en: Meow a few times to show gratitude

options.pinyin: děng lǎorén jiào tāmen
options.text_en: Wait for the old man to call them

questions.q_zh: 老人说「猫不会问你为什么难过」，他想表达什么？
questions.q_en: What does the old man mean by 'cats won't ask you why you're sad'?

options.pinyin: māo bù cōngmíng
options.text_en: Cats aren't smart

options.pinyin: tā gèng xǐhuan māo ér bú shì rén
options.text_en: He prefers cats to people

options.text: 猫的陪伴是无条件的、不需要解释的，这种简单正是他需要的
options.pinyin: māo de péibàn shì wú tiáojiàn de, bù xūyào jiěshì de, zhè zhǒng jiǎndān zhèng shì tā xūyào de
options.text_en: A cat's companionship is unconditional, requires no explanation, and that simplicity is exactly what he needs

options.text: 他不想跟别人谈论自己的生活
options.pinyin: tā bù xiǎng gēn biéren tánlùn zìjǐ de shēnghuó
options.text_en: He doesn't want to discuss his life with others

questions.q_zh: 作者最后的感悟是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the author's final insight?

options.pinyin: yīnggāi lǐngyǎng liúlàng māo
options.text_en: People should adopt stray cats

options.text: 不是老人在喂猫，而是猫在陪老人
options.pinyin: bú shì lǎorén zài wèi māo, ér shì māo zài péi lǎorén
options.text_en: It's not the old man feeding the cats—it's the cats keeping him company

options.text: 老人应该搬去和儿女住
options.pinyin: lǎorén yīnggāi bān qù hé érnǚ zhù
options.text_en: The old man should move in with his children

options.text: 流浪猫需要更多的食物
options.pinyin: liúlàng māo xūyào gèng duō de shíwù
options.text_en: Stray cats need more food

title: The Breakfast Cart Geography
text_zh: 我住的那条路上有三个早餐摊，彼此之间隔了不到两百米，但各自的气场完全不同。最东边的是一对河南夫妻，卖胡辣汤和油条，客人主要是建筑工地的工人和附近仓库的搬运工。中间那个是一位本地阿姨，卖粥和包子，来吃的大多是带孩子上学的家长和去菜场买菜的老人。最西边的是一个年轻小伙，卖咖啡和三明治，顾客基本上是附近写字楼的白领。三个早餐摊，三种客群，三种食物，三种说话的方式。河南夫妻嗓门大，跟客人聊天从来不用敬语；本地阿姨温温柔柔的，会多给熟客的孩子加一个小笼包；年轻小伙话不多，但咖啡做得很认真，拉花都不含糊。我有时候觉得，这三个早餐摊就是一部微型的社会学教科书。谁在哪里吃早餐，不仅说明了他吃什么，还说明了他是谁、他从哪里来、他要去哪里。一条两百米的路，浓缩了一座城市里至少三个阶层的清晨。而连接它们的，不过是同一条人行道和同一片早晨的阳光。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhù de nà tiáo lù shàng yǒu sān gè zǎocān tān, bǐcǐ zhī jiān gé le búdào liǎng bǎi mǐ, dàn gèzì de qìchǎng wánquán bùtóng. Zuì dōngbiān de shì yī duì Hénán fūqī, mài húlàtāng hé yóutiáo, kèrén zhǔyào shì jiànzhù gōngdì de gōngrén hé fùjìn cāngkù de bānyùngōng. Zhōngjiān nàge shì yī wèi běndì āyí, mài zhōu hé bāozi, lái chī de dàduō shì dài háizi shàngxué de jiāzhǎng hé qù càichǎng mǎi cài de lǎorén. Zuì xībiān de shì yī gè niánqīng xiǎohuǒ, mài kāfēi hé sānmíngzhì, gùkè jīběn shàng shì fùjìn xiězìlóu de báilǐng. Sān gè zǎocān tān, sān zhǒng kèqún, sān zhǒng shíwù, sān zhǒng shuōhuà de fāngshì. Hénán fūqī sǎngmén dà, gēn kèrén liáotiān cónglái búyòng jìngyǔ; běndì āyí wēnwēn róuróu de, huì duō gěi shúkè de háizi jiā yī gè xiǎo lóngbāo; niánqīng xiǎohuǒ huà bù duō, dàn kāfēi zuò de hěn rènzhēn, lāhuā dōu bù hánhu. Wǒ yǒu shíhou juéde, zhè sān gè zǎocān tān jiùshì yī bù wēixíng de shèhuìxué jiàokēshū. Shuí zài nǎlǐ chī zǎocān, bù jǐn shuōmíng le tā chī shénme, hái shuōmíng le tā shì shuí, tā cóng nǎlǐ lái, tā yào qù nǎlǐ. Yī tiáo liǎng bǎi mǐ de lù, nóngsuō le yī zuò chéngshì lǐ zhìshǎo sān gè jiēcéng de qīngchén. Ér liánjiē tāmen de, búguò shì tóngyī tiáo rénxíngdào hé tóngyī piàn zǎochen de yángguāng.
text_en: On the street where I live there are three breakfast stalls, less than two hundred meters apart, yet each with an entirely different atmosphere. At the eastern end is a Henan couple selling hot-and-sour soup and fried dough sticks; their customers are mainly construction workers and warehouse laborers. In the middle is a local auntie selling porridge and steamed buns; most of her diners are parents taking children to school and elderly people heading to the market. At the western end is a young man selling coffee and sandwiches; his customers are almost all white-collar workers from nearby office buildings. Three breakfast stalls, three customer groups, three cuisines, three styles of conversation. The Henan couple are loud and never use polite language with customers. The local auntie is gentle and soft-spoken, slipping an extra soup dumpling to a regular's child. The young man says little, but makes his coffee with great care—even the latte art is meticulous. Sometimes I feel these three stalls are a miniature sociology textbook. Where someone eats breakfast doesn't just tell you what they eat—it tells you who they are, where they come from, and where they're going. A two-hundred-meter stretch of road condenses the mornings of at least three social strata of a city. And what connects them is nothing more than the same sidewalk and the same morning sunlight.

questions.q_en: What does each of the three breakfast stalls sell?

options.pinyin: miàntiáo / jiǎozi / mǐfàn

options.pinyin: jiānbing / niúnǎi / cháyèdàn

options.text: 这条路应该铺更好的人行道

options.text: 虽然阶层不同，但所有人共享同一座城市的公共空间
options.pinyin: suīrán jiēcéng bùtóng, dàn suǒyǒu rén gòngxiǎng tóngyī zuò chéngshì de gōnggòng kōngjiān

options.text: 早上的阳光对健康有益

options.pinyin: sān gè tānwèi jùlí tài jìn le
options.text_en: The three stalls are too close together

title: The Rain on the Window
text_zh: 下雨天的时候，我喜欢坐在窗边看雨水沿着玻璃往下流。每一颗雨滴落在玻璃上，都会停顿一下，像是在犹豫往哪个方向走。然后，它会选择一条路线往下滑，中途可能会遇到另一颗雨滴，两颗合成一颗更大的，速度突然加快。有时候两颗雨滴的路线明明很近，却始终没有碰到一起，各自沿着自己的轨迹滑到窗框边消失了。我小时候经常玩一个游戏：选两颗雨滴，打赌哪一颗先滑到底。后来长大了，看雨滴的时候想的东西不一样了。我开始觉得雨滴在玻璃上的路径和人的一生很像——你以为你在选择方向，其实是玻璃上看不见的纹路和灰尘在替你做决定。两个人的轨迹靠得再近，也不一定会相遇；而那些看起来毫不相干的雨滴，反而可能在某个拐弯处汇合。这样的想法当然有点矫情。但下雨天本来就是适合矫情的天气，不是吗？所以我允许自己在窗边坐一个下午，看那些小小的水滴，完成它们短暂而认真的旅程。
text_pinyin: Xiàyǔ tiān de shíhou, wǒ xǐhuan zuò zài chuāng biān kàn yǔshuǐ yánzhe bōli wǎng xià liú. Měi yī kē yǔdī luò zài bōli shàng, dōu huì tíngdùn yīxià, xiàng shì zài yóuyù wǎng nǎge fāngxiàng zǒu. Ránhòu, tā huì xuǎnzé yī tiáo lùxiàn wǎng xià huá, zhōngtú kěnéng huì yùdào lìng yī kē yǔdī, liǎng kē héchéng yī kē gèng dà de, sùdù tūrán jiākuài. Yǒu shíhou liǎng kē yǔdī de lùxiàn míngmíng hěn jìn, què shǐzhōng méiyǒu pèng dào yīqǐ, gèzì yánzhe zìjǐ de guǐjì huá dào chuāngkuàng biān xiāoshī le. Wǒ xiǎo shíhou jīngcháng wán yī gè yóuxì: xuǎn liǎng kē yǔdī, dǎdǔ nǎ yī kē xiān huá dào dǐ. Hòulái zhǎng dà le, kàn yǔdī de shíhou xiǎng de dōngxi bù yīyàng le. Wǒ kāishǐ juéde yǔdī zài bōli shàng de lùjìng hé rén de yīshēng hěn xiàng——nǐ yǐwéi nǐ zài xuǎnzé fāngxiàng, qíshí shì bōli shàng kàn bú jiàn de wénlù hé huīchén zài tì nǐ zuò juédìng. Liǎng gè rén de guǐjì kào de zài jìn, yě bù yīdìng huì xiāngyù; ér nàxiē kàn qǐlái háo bù xiāngān de yǔdī, fǎn'ér kěnéng zài mǒu gè guǎiwān chù huìhé. Zhèyàng de xiǎngfǎ dāngrán yǒudiǎn jiǎoqíng. Dàn xiàyǔ tiān běnlái jiùshì shìhé jiǎoqíng de tiānqì, bú shì ma? Suǒyǐ wǒ yǔnxǔ zìjǐ zài chuāng biān zuò yī gè xiàwǔ, kàn nàxiē xiǎo xiǎo de shuǐdī, wánchéng tāmen duǎnzàn ér rènzhēn de lǚchéng.
text_en: On rainy days I like to sit by the window and watch raindrops flow down the glass. Each drop that lands on the glass pauses for a moment, as if hesitating about which direction to go. Then it chooses a path and slides down, perhaps meeting another drop midway—two merging into one larger drop, suddenly accelerating. Sometimes two drops take paths that are very close yet never touch, each sliding along its own trajectory to the window frame, where it disappears. As a child I often played a game: pick two raindrops and bet which one reaches the bottom first. Growing up, I began thinking differently when watching raindrops. I started feeling that a raindrop's path on glass is much like a person's life—you think you're choosing your direction, but actually it's the invisible texture and dust on the glass making the decisions for you. Two people's trajectories can be as close as possible yet never meet; while drops that seem completely unrelated might converge at some unexpected turn. Thoughts like this are admittedly a bit sentimental. But rainy days were made for sentimentality, weren't they? So I allow myself to sit by the window for an afternoon, watching those tiny droplets complete their brief but earnest journeys.

questions.q_en: How does the author's childhood way of watching raindrops differ from adulthood?

options.text: 小时候不喜欢下雨，长大后喜欢了

options.text: 小时候害怕雨，长大后不怕了

options.pinyin: rénshēng xiàng yǔdī yīyàng duǎnzàn
options.text_en: Life is as brief as a raindrop

options.text: 你以为在选择方向，但看不见的因素在替你做决定；相遇与否也不完全取决于距离

options.text_en: You think you're choosing direction, but invisible factors decide for you; meeting or not doesn't entirely depend on distance

options.text: 人生应该像雨滴一样自由
options.pinyin: rénshēng yīnggāi xiàng yǔdī yīyàng zìyóu

options.text_en: Self-deprecating but forgiving—allowing himself to be emotional occasionally

title: The Dialect I Can Understand but Not Speak
title_zh: 听得懂但说不出的方言
text_zh: 我爸是四川人，我妈是北京人，我在北京长大。从小到大，爸爸跟妈妈说普通话，但跟爷爷奶奶打电话的时候说四川话。我听了二十多年，四川话基本上能听懂，但说不出来。有一次回老家过年，亲戚们都说四川话，我全程都能听懂，但回答的时候只能用普通话。表哥笑我说：「你这个假四川人，耳朵是四川的，嘴巴是北京的。」我当时笑了，但心里有一种奇怪的感觉。后来我想明白了：听得懂一种语言但说不出来，其实是一种特殊的位置——你站在那个文化的边界上，看得见里面的人在做什么，听得见他们在说什么，但你进不去。你永远是一个有通行证的旁观者。这种感觉不完全是失落。它也给了我一种独特的视角：因为进不去，反而能看到里面的人看不到的东西。就像翻译永远比原文多出一层理解——那层理解来自距离。
text_pinyin: Wǒ bà shì Sìchuānrén, wǒ mā shì Běijīngrén, wǒ zài Běijīng zhǎng dà. Cóng xiǎo dào dà, bàba gēn māma shuō pǔtōnghuà, dàn gēn yéye nǎinai dǎ diànhuà de shíhou shuō Sìchuānhuà. Wǒ tīng le èrshí duō nián, Sìchuānhuà jīběn shàng néng tīng dǒng, dàn shuō bù chūlái. Yǒu yī cì huí lǎojiā guònián, qīnqimen dōu shuō Sìchuānhuà, wǒ quánchéng dōu néng tīng dǒng, dàn huídá de shíhou zhǐ néng yòng pǔtōnghuà. Biǎogē xiào wǒ shuō: 「Nǐ zhège jiǎ Sìchuānrén, ěrduo shì Sìchuān de, zuǐba shì Běijīng de.」Wǒ dāngshí xiào le, dàn xīnlǐ yǒu yī zhǒng qíguài de gǎnjué. Hòulái wǒ xiǎng míngbai le: tīng de dǒng yī zhǒng yǔyán dàn shuō bù chūlái, qíshí shì yī zhǒng tèshū de wèizhì——nǐ zhàn zài nàge wénhuà de biānjiè shàng, kàn de jiàn lǐmiàn de rén zài zuò shénme, tīng de jiàn tāmen zài shuō shénme, dàn nǐ jìn bú qù. Nǐ yǒngyuǎn shì yī gè yǒu tōngxíngzhèng de pángguānzhě. Zhè zhǒng gǎnjué bù wánquán shì shīluò. Tā yě gěi le wǒ yī zhǒng dútè de shìjiǎo: yīnwèi jìn bú qù, fǎn'ér néng kàn dào lǐmiàn de rén kàn bú dào de dōngxi. Jiù xiàng fānyì yǒngyuǎn bǐ yuánwén duō chū yī céng lǐjiě——nà céng lǐjiě lái zì jùlí.
text_en: My father is from Sichuan, my mother from Beijing. I grew up in Beijing. All my life, Dad spoke Mandarin with Mom, but switched to Sichuanese when calling Grandpa and Grandma. After listening for over twenty years, I can basically understand Sichuanese but can't speak it. Once when I went back to the hometown for Spring Festival, all the relatives spoke Sichuanese. I understood everything throughout, but could only answer in Mandarin. My cousin laughed: 'You fake Sichuanese person—your ears are from Sichuan, but your mouth is from Beijing.' I laughed at the time, but felt something strange inside. Later I figured it out: being able to understand a language but not speak it is actually a special position—you stand at the border of that culture, able to see what the people inside are doing, hear what they're saying, but you can't enter. You're forever an onlooker with a pass. This feeling isn't entirely one of loss. It also gave me a unique perspective: because I can't enter, I can see things that the people inside cannot. Just as translation always carries one more layer of understanding than the original—that layer comes from distance.
